title: 2 Chronicles 1
subtitle: Solomon at Gibeon — Ask What You Will
prayer_prompt: As Solomon asks God for wisdom at Gibeon and receives 'wisdom and knowledge,' consider asking God what you would request if He offered you anything — and whether your answer reveals where your heart truly is.

refs.note: The Kings parallel. The Chronicler omits Solomon’s dream and the judgment of the two prostitutes, keeping only the divine encounter.

notes.note: A thousand offerings teaches that generosity in worship should precede requests from God. Solomon gives extravagantly before asking anything. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Bezalel’s bronze altar (Exod 38:1–7) provides the direct Mosaic pedigree for Solomon’s worship. The altar at Gibeon is the altar from Sinai. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: Solomon goes to Gibeon where the tabernacle and bronze altar of Bezalel are located. He offers a thousand burnt offerings. The Chronicler emphasises the legitimacy of Gibeon worship — the tabernacle is there, even though the ark is in Jerusalem. Solomon’s first act as king is not political consolidation (as in 1 Kings 2) but worship. The Chronicler’s Solomon begins at the altar, not the throne.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew qōḏeš (holiness) denotes separation for divine purpose. In the context of temple worship, it marks the distinction between common and sacred — a boundary that defines Israel's entire liturgical life. The Chronicler's emphasis on holiness reflects his concern for proper worship as the foundation of national life.

notes.note: Solomon's wisdom was a direct gift from God, granted in response to humble petition rather than selfish ambition (cf. James 1:5). Yet even Solomon's legendary wisdom proved insufficient apart from sustained obedience. The truly wise king whom Solomon foreshadows is Christ, in whom 'are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' (Col 2:3).

Solomon's reign — in both its glory and its eventual decline — serves as a living parable of the danger of spiritual compromise. The wisest man who ever lived was undone by disobedience to clear commands of Scripture (Deut 17:16-17). If Solomon could fall, no believer dare presume upon their own strength. Sustained faithfulness requires sustained dependence on God's grace.

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

sections.header: Verses 7–17 — “Ask for Whatever You Want”; Solomon Chooses Wisdom

refs.note: The Kings parallel includes the dream format and is more expansive on God’s pleasure at Solomon’s request.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: hokmah umaddaʿ — “wisdom and knowledge.” The pairing is unique to Chronicles; 1 Kgs 3:9 uses lēb shōmēaʿ (“a discerning heart”). The Chronicler intellectualises the request. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: God appears to Solomon at night: “Ask for whatever you want me to give you.” Solomon asks for wisdom and knowledge to govern. God grants these plus wealth and honour “such as no king who was before you ever had and none after you will have.” Solomon returns to Jerusalem and his wealth is described: 1,400 chariots, 12,000 horses, silver “as common as stones.” The Chronicler’s version omits the dream framework and the famous judgment scene (1 Kgs 3:16–28) — Solomon’s wisdom is asserted, not demonstrated.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew mālaḵ (to reign) carries the weight of divinely delegated authority. In the Chronicler's theology, every king reigns as God's representative — their faithfulness or failure directly affects the nation's destiny. The verb appears at the critical transitions that shape Israel's monarchic history.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVand Solomon and the whole assembly went to the high place at Gibeon, for God’s tent of meeting was there, which Moses the Lord’s servant had made in the wilderness.ESVAnd Solomon, and all the assembly with him, went to the high place that was at Gibeon, for the tent of meeting of God, which Moses the servant of the LORD had made in the wilderness, was there.NIVSolomon went up to the bronze altar before the Lord in the tent of meeting and offered a thousand burnt offerings on it.ESVAnd Solomon went up there to the bronze altar before the LORD, which was at the tent of meeting, and offered a thousand burnt offerings on it.

themes.note: Solomon at Gibeon — Ask What You Will

coaching.tip: Unlike 1 Kings, which opens with David's decline, 2 Chronicles begins with Solomon already established. The Chronicler skips the messy succession crisis. This is deliberate — Chronicles focuses on temple and worship, not political intrigue. Solomon's first act is a pilgrimage to Gibeon 'because God's tent of meeting was there.' Worship precedes wisdom.

God's offer to Solomon — 'Ask what I shall give you' — is a test of heart. Solomon asks for wisdom to govern, not wealth or long life. God's response: 'Because this was in your heart... I give you what you have not asked.' The principle appears throughout Scripture: seek first the kingdom, and these things will be added (Matt 6:33).
